tasks.title: Disable auto-commit and implement explicit transactions in DatabaseConfig
tasks.description: Modify database configuration to disable auto-commit and enable explicit transaction management for atomic operations.
tasks.details: Update DatabaseConfig.kt to set isAutoCommit = false in HikariConfig. Use Kotlin coroutines withContext(Dispatchers.IO) for database operations. Implement a suspend extension function transactionSuspend on DSLContext that wraps operations in a transaction block using jOOQ's transaction method.
tasks.testStrategy: Verify that save(), update(), and deleteById() repository methods execute within a single transaction by simulating failures between operations and ensuring no partial commits occur. Use integration tests with rollback verification.

tasks.title: Refactor repository methods to use transactionSuspend for atomic operations
tasks.description: Update repository methods save(), update(), and deleteById() to wrap all database calls within transactionSuspend to ensure atomicity.
tasks.details: In repository implementations like CityRepositoryImpl and RestroomRepositoryImpl, wrap all related DB calls (e.g., insert and select) inside ctx.transactionSuspend { txCtx -> ... } to ensure they execute in one transaction. Remove any auto-commit reliance.
tasks.testStrategy: Unit and integration tests to confirm that partial failures do not leave inconsistent data. Test rollback behavior on exceptions within transactionSuspend blocks.

tasks.description: Implement an extension function or property to provide coordinate fields projection to avoid repeated code in repositories.

tasks.testStrategy: Code review to ensure all coordinate field usages are replaced. Unit tests for repository methods to verify correct fields are selected.

tasks.title: Add fetchCount parameter to executePaginated and optimize count queries
tasks.description: Modify executePaginated to accept a fetchCount boolean parameter to optionally skip COUNT queries for performance optimization.
tasks.details: Update QueryExecutor.kt to propagate fetchCount parameter. For endpoints like /nearest, call executePaginated with fetchCount=false to avoid expensive COUNT(*) queries. Consider approximate counts or caching for large tables in future iterations.
tasks.testStrategy: Benchmark response times with fetchCount true vs false on large datasets. Verify pagination behavior remains correct when count is skipped.

tasks.title: Implement health check endpoints with database connectivity verification
tasks.description: Create health check endpoints (/health, /health/ready, /health/live) that include database connectivity status, implemented in HealthController.kt with routing configured in RoutingConfig.kt, and provide comprehensive unit tests.
tasks.details:
1. Create HealthController.kt to define three endpoints: /health, /health/ready, and /health/live. Each endpoint should return appropriate HTTP status and JSON indicating service health.

2. Implement a database connectivity check within the health endpoints by executing a lightweight query or ping against the database using the existing database configuration (ensuring it uses explicit transactions as per Task 1).

3. Integrate these endpoints into the application's routing by adding routes in RoutingConfig.kt, ensuring they are accessible and follow REST conventions.

4. Structure the health responses to comply with common microservice health check standards, distinguishing readiness and liveness checks (e.g., readiness includes database connectivity, liveness confirms service is running).

5. Write unit tests for HealthController to verify each endpoint returns correct status codes and payloads under normal and simulated failure conditions (e.g., database down).

6. Follow Kotlin coroutine best practices for asynchronous database calls and HTTP handling.

7. Ensure logging and error handling are consistent with existing project standards.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Unit test each health endpoint to assert HTTP 200 OK with expected JSON when the database is reachable.

2. Simulate database connectivity failure and verify endpoints return appropriate failure status (e.g., HTTP 503 Service Unavailable) and error details.

3. Test routing configuration to confirm endpoints are correctly registered and accessible.

4. Perform integration tests to confirm health endpoints reflect real database connectivity status.

5. Code review to ensure adherence to project coding standards and proper use of coroutines and transactions.

tasks.title: Implement Local-First Task Management for Cursor Agent
tasks.description: Enable Cursor Agent to perform all read operations directly from the local tasks.json file and cache task context in memory during the session, minimizing API calls to Task Master.
tasks.details: Implement file system reading of `.taskmaster/tasks/tasks.json` for fetching task lists, filtering by status, priority, and dependencies. Parse the `details` field of tasks and subtasks for context. Cache this context in memory for session duration to avoid repeated file reads. Restrict API calls to only write operations such as `update_subtask`, `add_task`, and `expand_task`. Ensure all implementation details are stored within the `details` field of tasks and subtasks. All rule files and documentation have been consolidated and optimized: duplicate and redundant files have been removed, integration rules merged into a single `taskmaster_integration.mdc`, and all references updated to point to the correct files. The context size has been significantly reduced while preserving all functionality.
tasks.testStrategy: Validate that all read operations do not trigger API calls by monitoring network requests. Test correct parsing and filtering of tasks from the local JSON file. Verify in-memory cache updates and consistency during session. Confirm that write operations still use API calls as expected. Ensure that the consolidated rule and documentation files are correctly referenced and that no legacy files are accessed.

tasks.title: Develop Automated Workflow Integration from User Task to Subtasks
tasks.description: Automate the entire workflow from receiving a new user task through generating and working on subtasks without explicit user mention of Task Master.
tasks.details: Implement a pipeline that upon receiving a new user task: 1) creates a structured task description, 2) formats it into a PRD, 3) calls `parse_prd` API to generate subtasks, 4) retrieves generated subtasks, and 5) proceeds to work strictly on these subtasks. Ensure this workflow triggers automatically and integrates seamlessly with Cursor Agent and Task Master.
tasks.testStrategy: Test with various new user tasks to confirm automatic creation of PRD and subtasks. Verify that subtasks are correctly generated and that the agent works only on these subtasks. Confirm no manual intervention or explicit Task Master mention is required.

tasks.title: Implement Context Preservation and Structured Logging
tasks.description: Ensure all implementation details and progress are logged in the `details` field using a structured format to enable full context preservation and recovery.
tasks.details: Use the `update_subtask` API to log progress and implementation details in a structured format including Plan, Files, Changes, Issues, Progress, and Next steps. Implement saving of research outputs via `research --save-file`. Enforce a maximum length of 50,000 characters for the `details` field. Structure logs to facilitate easy parsing and recovery.
tasks.testStrategy: Verify that all updates to subtasks include structured logs in the `details` field. Test that research files are saved correctly. Confirm that the length limit is respected and logs remain parseable. Simulate recovery scenarios to ensure context completeness.

tasks.title: Create Reusable Configuration Templates for Task Master and Cursor Agent
tasks.description: Develop configuration templates for Task Master and Cursor Agent to enable easy reuse and automatic application for new projects.
tasks.details:
Templates have been created and configurations updated for optimal reuse and automatic workflow:

- `.taskmaster/templates/agent-rules-template.mdc` - Agent integration rules

All templates are ready for reuse in new projects. Configuration is optimized for local-first strategy and automatic workflow.
tasks.testStrategy: Test template files for correctness and completeness. Validate automatic application of templates on new project setup. Confirm that Cursor Agent and Task Master operate with these configurations and that model settings are optimal.

tasks.title: Implement Fallback Recovery Mechanism Using Local Files
tasks.description: Ensure full recovery of task context and state from local files without any API calls in case of failures or restarts.
tasks.details: Develop recovery logic that reads `.taskmaster/tasks/tasks.json` and other local files to reconstruct the entire task and subtask state, including statuses, priorities, dependencies, and detailed logs. Confirm that this recovery works independently of any API calls, providing a complete picture of the current state for Cursor Agent and Task Master.
tasks.testStrategy: Simulate failure and restart scenarios. Verify that recovery from local files restores full context and state accurately. Confirm no API calls are made during recovery. Test consistency of recovered data with pre-failure state.

tasks.title: Optimize API Usage with Batched Write Operations
tasks.description: Reduce the number of API calls by batching updates and write operations to Task Master AI.
tasks.details: Implement batching logic for write operations such as `update_subtask`, `add_task`, and `expand_task`. Accumulate multiple updates in memory and send them in a single API call to minimize overhead and resource consumption. Ensure batching respects session context and does not delay critical updates excessively.
tasks.testStrategy: Measure API call frequency before and after batching implementation. Confirm that batched calls correctly update Task Master state without data loss. Test system responsiveness and consistency under various workloads.

tasks.title: Integrate Economical AI Models for Main and Fallback Operations
tasks.description: Configure and integrate cost-effective AI models (sonar for main/research, gemini-2.0-flash for fallback) to optimize resource usage.
tasks.details: Set up model selection logic to use the sonar model for main and research tasks and gemini-2.0-flash as a fallback model. Ensure seamless switching between models based on availability and task type. Update configuration templates to reflect these model preferences.
tasks.testStrategy: Test model selection under normal and fallback conditions. Validate that tasks use the correct models as per configuration. Monitor resource usage and cost metrics to confirm optimization.

tasks.title: Implement Filtering and Status Checking for Local Task Operations
tasks.description: Enable efficient local filtering of tasks by status, priority, and dependencies, and implement subtask status checks without API calls.
tasks.details: Develop functions to filter tasks read from `tasks.json` based on status, priority, and dependencies. Implement logic to check and update subtask statuses locally by parsing the `details` field. Ensure these operations are performant and accurate to support local-first management and recovery.
tasks.testStrategy: Test filtering accuracy with various task datasets. Validate subtask status detection and updates locally. Confirm no API calls occur during these operations. Benchmark performance for large task sets.

tasks.title: Collect Changed Files and Task Data
tasks.description: Gather detailed information about changed files and task details for generating the commit message, using a structured and documented approach.
tasks.details:
Implement a function or script that performs the following five-step process:
1. Verify if the current directory is a git repository using appropriate git commands.
2. Collect the list of changed files using `git status --porcelain` with detailed parsing to identify file states.
3. Retrieve task data (ID, title, description) locally from `tasks.json` without relying on external APIs.
4. Implement a function `generateCommitMessage` that accepts parameters for changed files and task data to produce a structured commit message.
5. Document all steps, commands, and function usage comprehensively in `git_commit.mdc` for maintainability and clarity.

Include example git commands for repository verification and file collection as part of the implementation documentation.
<info added on 2025-11-30T17:18:42.913Z>
Работа над задачей 37 завершена. В документацию `git_commit.mdc` добавлена детальная информация по всем пяти шагам процесса генерации commit message:

1. **Проверка Git репозитория**:  
   - Примеры команд для проверки наличия git репозитория:  
     - `git rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree`  
     - `git status`  
   - Альтернативные методы проверки:  
     - Проверка наличия директории `.git`  
     - Использование `git rev-parse --git-dir`  
   - Пример реализации на Kotlin с обработкой ошибок:  
     ```kotlin
     fun isGitRepository(): Boolean {
         return try {
             val process = Runtime.getRuntime().exec("git rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree")
             process.waitFor() == 0
         } catch (e: Exception) {
             false
         }
     }
     ```

2. **Парсинг git status --porcelain**:  
   - Описание формата вывода команды `git status --porcelain`  
   - Статусные коды:  
     - `M` — модифицированный файл  
     - `A` — добавленный файл  
     - `D` — удалённый файл  
     - `R` — переименованный файл (формат: `R oldfile -> newfile`)  
     - `C` — скопированный файл  
     - `??` — неотслеживаемый файл  
   - Реализация функции `parseGitStatusPorcelain` на Kotlin с обработкой всех статусов, включая переименованные и неотслеживаемые файлы  
   - Примеры bash-скриптов для парсинга вывода:  
     ```bash
     git status --porcelain | while read line; do
         status=$(echo "$line" | cut -c1-2)
         file=$(echo "$line" | cut -c4-)
         echo "Status: $status, File: $file"
     done
     ```
   - Примеры вывода и ожидаемых результатов для различных сценариев

3. **Чтение данных задачи из tasks.json**:  
   - Реализация функции `readLocalTask` на Kotlin с поддержкой тегов  
   - Альтернативный метод прямого парсинга JSON с использованием библиотеки Jackson  
   - Примеры использования для извлечения id, title, description:  
     ```kotlin
     data class Task(val id: Int, val title: String, val description: String)
     fun readLocalTask(): Task {
         val json = File("tasks.json").readText()
         return jacksonObjectMapper().readValue(json, Task::class.java)
     }
     ```

4. **Дополнительные разделы**:  
   - **Command Reference**: Справочник всех команд workflow с примерами использования  
   - **Testing the Implementation**: Примеры тестирования парсинга и проверки git репозитория, включая сценарии с различными статусами файлов и ошибками

Вся документация содержит детальные примеры команд, реализации и тестирования для всех пяти шагов процесса генерации commit message.

tasks.title: Configure jOOQ Code Generation Dependency for Kotlin Compilation and Validate Imports/Output Location
tasks.description: Ensure jOOQ classes are generated before Kotlin compilation and verify all jOOQ imports and output locations are correct.
tasks.details:
After recent changes, jOOQ-generated classes are not being generated automatically after a clean build, causing test compilation failures. To resolve this:

1. Update the Gradle build configuration to make the `compileKotlin` task depend on the `generateJooq` task. This ensures that jOOQ code generation always runs before Kotlin compilation.

   Example (Gradle Kotlin DSL):
   ```kotlin
   tasks.compileKotlin { dependsOn(tasks.named("generateJooq")) }
   ```

2. Verify that the jOOQ code generation configuration specifies the correct output directory (typically `build/generated-sources/jooq` or similar) and that this directory is included in the source sets for compilation.

3. Check all imports in test and main code that reference jOOQ-generated classes. Ensure they match the actual package and class names generated by jOOQ.

4. Confirm that the jOOQ configuration in `build.gradle.kts` or equivalent specifies the correct schema and target package, and that the generated classes appear in the expected location after running the build.

5. If using custom generator strategies or extensions, ensure they are correctly referenced and available on the classpath during code generation.

6. Run a clean build and verify that jOOQ classes are generated and available for compilation.

7. Ensure that tasks such as `compileKotlin`, `test`, and `build` are properly conditioned to run after the jOOQ code generation task, especially when using containerized databases for code generation.

8. Set up any additional tooling tasks (e.g., ktlint) to run after jOOQ code generation using `mustRunAfter` or similar mechanisms to avoid race conditions.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Run a clean build (`./gradlew clean build`) and confirm that jOOQ-generated classes are present in the expected output directory.
2. Verify that all tests compile and run without errors related to missing jOOQ classes.
3. Check that all jOOQ imports in the codebase are correct and that generated classes are used as expected.
4. Confirm that the `compileKotlin` task does not start until after the `generateJooq` task has completed successfully.
5. Ensure that containerized database tasks are correctly conditioned to run before code generation and that all dependent tasks (compileKotlin, test, build) are executed after code generation.
6. Validate that tooling tasks like ktlint run after code generation and do not interfere with the build process.
